1.1: The LORD called Moses by name and spoke to him from the tent of meeting:
1.2: Tell the Israelites: When any of you wants to bring an offering to the LORD, bring an animal from the herd or the flock.
1.3: If the offering is a whole burnt offering from the herd, it must be a flawless male, brought willingly to the entrance of the tent of meeting where the LORD will accept it.
1.4: He lays his hand on the animal's head — transferring his identity to the offering — and the LORD accepts it to cover his guilt.
1.5: He slaughters the animal before the LORD; the priests carry the blood and dash it around every side of the altar at the tent entrance — blood that marks the boundary between death and life.
1.6: He skins the carcass and cuts it into portions.
1.7: Aaron's sons kindle the altar fire and stack the wood in order on it.
1.8: The priests lay out all the portions — head, fat, and body pieces — in proper order on the burning wood.
1.9: The internal organs and legs are washed clean with water; then the priest burns the whole animal on the altar — a fragrant offering wholly given to the LORD.
1.10: If the offering comes from the flock — sheep or goat — it must be a flawless male.
1.11: It is slaughtered at the north side of the altar — the LORD's side — and the priests dash its blood around the altar.
1.12: He cuts it into portions, head and fat included, and the priest arrays them on the burning wood.
1.13: The entrails and legs are washed, then the priest burns everything on the altar — a whole offering, fragrant and wholly given to the LORD.
1.14: If someone's means allows only a bird for a burnt offering to the LORD, let it be a turtledove or a young pigeon.
1.15: The priest brings the bird to the altar, wrings off its head and burns it; the blood is squeezed out along the altar's side.
1.16: The crop and feathers are discarded in the ash pit on the east side of the altar.
1.17: He splits it open by its wings without tearing it apart, and the priest burns it whole on the altar — a fragrant offering wholly surrendered to the LORD.

2.1: When someone wants to bring a grain offering to the LORD, let it be of fine flour: oil drizzled over it, frankincense laid upon it.
2.2: He brings it to the priests; a priest takes a representative handful — the flour, oil, and all the frankincense — and burns it on the altar as a token of the whole, fragrant before the LORD.
2.3: The rest of the grain offering belongs to Aaron and his sons — a most holy share of what has been offered to the LORD.
2.4: If your grain offering is oven-baked, it must be unleavened — either flat loaves of fine flour kneaded with oil, or thin wafers spread with oil.
2.5: If the offering is cooked on a flat griddle, use fine flour mixed with oil, and make it without leaven.
2.6: Break it into pieces and drizzle oil over it — this is a grain offering.
2.7: If the offering is made in a covered pan, prepare it from fine flour and oil.
2.8: Whatever form it takes, bring the grain offering to the LORD; the priest receives it and takes it to the altar.
2.9: The priest lifts out the token portion and burns it on the altar, its aroma rising to the LORD.
2.10: The rest belongs to Aaron and the priests — their sacred portion from the offerings given to the LORD.
2.11: Never put yeast or honey into any grain offering burned to the LORD — they ferment and corrupt, and only what is pure may be given to him in fire.
2.12: Firstfruits offerings belong to the LORD, but they are not burned on the altar — they are set apart for another purpose.
2.13: Salt every grain offering without exception — salt is the sign of the covenant between you and your God. The covenant cannot be offered without its seal: salt.
2.14: When you bring a firstfruits grain offering to the LORD, use freshly harvested grain — roasted over fire and coarsely ground from ripe ears.
2.15: Drizzle oil on it and place frankincense on top — it is a grain offering to the LORD.
2.16: The priest burns the token portion — grain, oil, and all the frankincense — as a fragrant offering to the LORD.

3.1: When the offering is a fellowship sacrifice from the herd — whether male or female — it must be flawless, presented before the LORD.
3.2: He lays his hand on the animal's head, then slaughters it at the tent entrance; the priests throw the blood against every side of the altar.
3.3: He presents to the LORD the fatty portions of the fellowship offering: the fat surrounding the internal organs and all the fat within,
3.4: the two kidneys and their surrounding fat at the flanks, and the liver's fatty lobe — all removed along with the kidneys.
3.5: The priests burn these portions on the altar, laid on the burnt offering already burning there — a fragrant offering given to the LORD.
3.6: If the fellowship offering comes from the flock — sheep or goat, male or female — it must be without flaw.
3.7: If it is a lamb, he presents it before the LORD,
3.8: lays his hand on its head, slaughters it before the tent of meeting, and the priests throw the blood around every side of the altar.
3.9: From the lamb he presents the LORD's portion: the fat — including the entire fatty tail, removed near the backbone — and all the fat around the internal organs.
3.10: the two kidneys with their flank fat, and the liver's fatty lobe removed with the kidneys.
3.11: The priest burns it all on the altar — this is the food offered to the LORD.
3.12: If it is a goat, he brings it before the LORD,
3.13: lays his hand on its head, slaughters it before the tent of meeting, and the priests throw the blood around all sides of the altar.
3.14: He presents from it the LORD's portion — a fire offering — beginning with all the fat around the internal organs:
3.15: the two kidneys and their flank fat, and the fatty lobe of the liver removed with the kidneys.
3.16: The priest burns it all on the altar — a fragrant offering, the food of the LORD. All fat belongs to the LORD alone.
3.17: For all generations and wherever you live, this rule stands without exception: you must not eat fat, and you must not eat blood.

4.2: Tell the Israelites: If any person commits an unintentional sin — doing something the LORD has forbidden without fully knowing it —
4.3: if it is the anointed priest who sins — and his sin spreads guilt throughout the community — he must bring a flawless young bull to the LORD as a sin offering.
4.4: He brings the bull to the entrance of the tent of meeting, lays his hand on its head — bearing the weight of the sin himself — and slaughters it before the LORD.
4.5: The anointed priest takes some of the bull's blood and brings it into the tent of meeting.
4.6: He dips his finger in the blood and flicks it seven times toward the curtain of the inner sanctuary — before the LORD himself.
4.7: He puts blood on the horns of the incense altar before the LORD inside the tent, and pours the remaining blood at the foot of the burnt-offering altar at the tent entrance — blood marking every sacred boundary.
4.8: He removes all the fatty portions from the sin-offering bull: the fat covering the entrails and all the fat within,
4.9: the two kidneys with their flank fat, and the liver's fatty lobe removed with them —
4.10: the same portions taken for the fellowship offering. The priest burns them all on the altar.
4.11: But the hide, flesh, head, legs, entrails, and dung of the bull —
4.12: he carries the whole carcass to the clean ash-disposal site outside the camp and burns it there on a wood fire. It must burn where the ashes are poured out.
4.13: If the whole community of Israel sins unintentionally — the whole assembly failing together, without anyone seeing the full picture — violating one of the LORD's prohibitions and incurring guilt —
4.14: once they discover the sin, the whole community brings a young bull as a sin offering and presents it before the tent of meeting.
4.15: The community elders lay their hands on the bull's head on behalf of the whole assembly, and the bull is slaughtered before the LORD.
4.16: The anointed priest brings blood from the bull into the tent of meeting,
4.17: dips his finger in the blood and flicks it seven times before the LORD's presence at the veil.
4.18: He puts blood on the horns of the altar inside the tent before the LORD, then pours the rest at the foot of the burnt-offering altar at the tent entrance.
4.19: He strips off all the fat and burns it on the altar.
4.20: The entire procedure follows what was prescribed for the priest's own sin offering. The priest makes atonement for the community, and they are forgiven.
4.21: The carcass is taken outside the camp and burned just like the first — it is the community's sin offering.
4.22: When a ruler sins unintentionally against a command of the LORD his God — doing what must not be done — and realizes his guilt,
4.23: when the sin he committed comes to light, he brings a flawless male goat as his offering.
4.24: He lays his hand on the goat's head, then slaughters it where burnt offerings are killed — before the LORD. It is a sin offering.
4.25: The priest takes blood on his finger, applies it to the altar horns, and pours the rest at the altar's base.
4.26: All the fat is burned on the altar, just as with the fellowship offering. The priest makes atonement for the ruler's sin, and he is forgiven.
4.27: If any ordinary member of the community sins unintentionally against any of the LORD's commands — doing what must not be done — and realizes his guilt,
4.28: when the sin comes to light, he brings a flawless female goat as his sin offering.
4.29: He lays his hand on its head and slaughters it at the place of burnt offerings.
4.30: The priest dabs blood on the altar horns with his finger and pours the rest at the base.
4.31: He removes all the fat as with the fellowship offering, and the priest burns it — a pleasing aroma to the LORD. Atonement is made, and the person is forgiven.
4.32: If the offering is a lamb rather than a goat, it must be a flawless female.
4.33: He lays his hand on its head and slaughters it as a sin offering at the place of burnt offerings.
4.34: The priest applies blood to the altar horns and pours the rest at the altar's base.
4.35: All fat is removed as with the fellowship lamb; the priest burns it on the altar along with the other offerings. Atonement is made for the sin, and the person is forgiven.

5.1: If someone hears a public oath demanding testimony and stays silent — though they saw or knew something — they bear the guilt of their silence.
5.2: Or if someone touches something ritually unclean — any dead unclean animal, domestic or wild, or crawling creature — even unknowingly, when the uncleanness becomes apparent, they are guilty.
5.3: Or if someone touches human ritual impurity of any kind without knowing it, when they realize it they are guilty.
5.4: Or if someone swears impulsively — pledging to do either harm or good — and then forgets about it, when they remember they are guilty.
5.5: When guilt is realized in any of these cases, the person must confess the specific sin openly —
5.6: then bring a female from the flock — lamb or female goat — as a sin offering. The priest makes atonement, and the guilt is covered.
5.7: If a lamb is beyond someone's means, they bring two turtledoves or two young pigeons — one for the sin offering, the other for a burnt offering. The law bends to meet the poor.
5.8: He brings both to the priest, who prepares the sin offering first: wringing the head partly from the neck without separating it.
5.9: He sprinkles the blood against the altar's side; the remainder drains down to the base. This is the sin offering.
5.10: The second bird is offered as a burnt offering by the proper procedure. Atonement is made and the person is forgiven.
5.11: If even two birds are too much, a tenth of an ephah of fine flour serves as a sin offering — no oil, no frankincense; it is a sin offering, not a grain offering. God makes provision for the poorest.
5.12: The priest takes a representative handful, burns it on the altar with the other offerings — it is a sin offering.
5.13: Atonement is made for whichever sin was committed, and forgiveness follows. The rest of the flour belongs to the priest as his portion, like any grain offering.
5.15: When someone inadvertently misuses any of the LORD's sacred things — trespassing on what has been set apart — they bring a flawless ram as a guilt offering, its worth assessed in sanctuary shekels. The offense was against the holy; the restitution must match.
5.16: He pays full restitution for what was misused from the holy things, adding a fifth of the value — and the priest makes atonement with the guilt-offering ram. The debt is paid; the person is forgiven.
5.17: If someone sins against any of the LORD's commands without knowing it, their ignorance does not remove the guilt — when awareness comes, so does accountability.
5.18: He brings a flawless ram, assessed at proper value, as a guilt offering; the priest makes atonement for the mistake, and forgiveness follows.
5.19: This is a guilt offering. The debt to the LORD is real, even when the offense was unintended.

6.2: When someone wrongs their neighbor and thereby wrongs the LORD — by lying about a deposit, a pledge, or stolen property, or by defrauding them —
6.3: or has found something that was lost and lied about it, sealing the lie with a false oath — in any of these —
6.4: once guilt is acknowledged, full restitution is required: return the stolen goods, the fraudulent gain, the entrusted deposit, the found property —
6.5: everything about which he swore falsely — returned in full plus twenty percent, paid back to the rightful owner on the very day atonement is made.
6.6: Then he brings his guilt offering to the LORD: a flawless ram at assessed value, given to the priest.
6.7: The priest makes atonement before the LORD, and forgiveness follows — for all the wrongs done against the neighbor, and through them, against God.
6.9: Give Aaron and his sons this instruction about the burnt offering: the offering stays on the altar hearth all through the night until morning. The altar fire must never go out.
6.10: In the morning the priest dresses in his full linen vestments and removes the ashes left by the burning — setting them beside the altar.
6.11: He changes into ordinary clothes and carries the ashes to the clean disposal place outside the camp.
6.12: The altar fire must burn without ceasing. Every morning the priest feeds it with fresh wood, arranges the day's burnt offering on it, and burns the fat portions of the fellowship offerings — the fire is never allowed to die.
6.13: Let the fire on the altar burn always — day and night, without ceasing. It must never go out.
6.14: This is the instruction for the grain offering. Aaron's sons present it before the LORD at the altar.
6.15: The priest takes a representative handful — flour, oil, and all the frankincense — and burns it on the altar as the token portion, a fragrant offering to the LORD.
6.16: Aaron and his sons eat the rest — without leaven, in the holy courtyard of the tent of meeting. Sacred food must be eaten in a sacred space.
6.17: No yeast. I have given this to them as their permanent portion of my fire offerings — it is most holy, ranked with the sin offering and guilt offering.
6.18: Any male in Aaron's line may eat it — a permanent entitlement across all generations. Whoever touches these holy offerings is made holy by the contact.
6.20: On the day a priest is anointed, this is the grain offering he must bring to the LORD: one-tenth of an ephah of fine flour, half offered in the morning, half at evening. It continues as a regular offering.
6.21: Prepare it on a griddle with oil — bring it well-mixed, baked into pieces. Offer it as a grain offering, a fragrant gift to the LORD.
6.22: Every high priest, upon taking office, must keep up this offering — a permanent obligation to the LORD, fully burned without any portion kept.
6.23: When the offering comes from a priest himself, it must be entirely burned — no portion belongs to the priest; it goes wholly to the LORD.
6.25: Tell Aaron and his sons: This is the rule for sin offerings. The sin offering is slaughtered in the same place as the burnt offering — before the LORD — and it is most holy.
6.26: The priest who performs the sin offering eats his portion — in the holy courtyard of the tent of meeting, nowhere else.
6.27: Whatever touches the sin-offering meat becomes holy — set apart. If blood is sprinkled on clothing, the garment must be washed right there in the holy court.
6.28: The clay pot used for cooking the sin offering must be smashed — the holiness has permeated it and cannot be cleaned away. A bronze pot can be scrubbed and rinsed.
6.29: Any male priest may eat it — it is most holy.
6.30: Any sin offering whose blood was brought inside the tent of meeting — used to make atonement in the inner sanctuary — may not be eaten. It must be burned entirely.

7.1: This is the ruling for the reparation offering: it is most holy.
7.2: The reparation offering is slaughtered in the same place as the burnt offering — its blood dashed against every side of the altar.
7.3: All its fat is offered: the fat tail and the fat layer covering the entrails,
7.4: the two kidneys with their loin fat, and the lobe of the liver removed along with the kidneys.
7.5: The priest burns all of it on the altar as a fire offering to the LORD — this is the reparation offering.
7.6: Any male priest may eat it — but only in the sacred precinct, for it is most holy.
7.7: Guilt offering and sin offering follow the same rule: the priest who performs the rite of atonement keeps the meat.
7.8: The priest who presents anyone's burnt offering gets to keep the animal's hide for himself.
7.9: Every grain offering — whether baked in an oven, cooked in a pan, or grilled on a griddle — belongs to the priest who presents it.
7.10: All grain offerings — whether mixed with oil or dry — are divided equally among Aaron's sons.
7.11: This is the ruling for the fellowship offering brought to the LORD.
7.12: If the offering expresses thanksgiving, it comes with three kinds of unleavened bread: loaves mixed with oil, wafers spread with oil, and fine-flour cakes blended with oil.
7.13: Alongside the unleavened breads, leavened loaves are also offered with the thanksgiving fellowship sacrifice.
7.14: One loaf from each type is set apart as a gift to the LORD and goes to the priest who dashes the fellowship blood against the altar.
7.15: The meat of a thanksgiving fellowship offering must be eaten the same day — nothing left over until morning.
7.16: If the fellowship offering fulfills a vow or is a spontaneous gift, the meat may be eaten that day and the next.
7.17: Whatever remains on the third day must be burned — it may not be eaten.
7.18: If anyone eats it on the third day, the sacrifice is rejected — it earns the offerer no credit. The meat has become offensive, and whoever eats it carries the guilt.
7.19: Meat that contacts anything impure must be burned, not eaten. Only those who are ritually clean may eat from the clean portions.
7.20: Anyone who eats from the LORD's fellowship offering while ritually impure shall be cut off from the community of Israel.
7.21: If someone has touched anything defiling — a human impurity, an unclean animal, or any detestable thing — and then eats from the LORD's fellowship offering, that person is cut off from Israel.
7.23: Tell the people of Israel: eat no fat — whether from cattle, sheep, or goat.
7.24: Fat from an animal that died naturally or was killed by a predator may be used for other purposes — but it may never be eaten.
7.25: Anyone who eats fat from an animal eligible for offering to the LORD is cut off from Israel.
7.26: Eat no blood — neither bird blood nor animal blood — in any place where you live.
7.27: Anyone who eats blood in any form shall be cut off from the people.
7.29: Tell Israel: the one who brings a fellowship offering must personally present his portion of it to the LORD.
7.30: He must carry it with his own hands — the fat and the breast together — so that the breast can be lifted and waved before the LORD.
7.31: The fat goes up in flames on the altar; the breast goes to Aaron and his sons.
7.32: The right thigh is given to the priest as a sacred gift from the fellowship offerings.
7.33: The priest who handles the blood and fat of the fellowship offering receives the right thigh as his share.
7.34: I have permanently set aside the wave breast and the raised thigh from Israel's fellowship offerings and given them to Aaron and his sons — a standing provision that never lapses.
7.35: This is the allotment set aside for Aaron and his sons from the LORD's offerings — their due from the day they were installed to serve as priests.
7.36: The LORD himself commanded Israel to provide this from the day he anointed them — a perpetual statute binding on every generation.
7.37: This concludes the law of the burnt offering, the grain offering, the sin offering, the reparation offering, the ordination offering, and the fellowship offering —
7.38: all of it commanded by the LORD to Moses on Mount Sinai, the day he charged Israel to bring their offerings to him in the Sinai wilderness.

8.2: Gather Aaron and his sons, the priestly vestments, the anointing oil, the bull for the sin offering, the two rams, and the basket of unleavened bread.
8.3: Summon the whole congregation to the entrance of the tent of meeting.
8.4: Moses did everything the LORD commanded, and the whole congregation gathered at the tent of meeting entrance.
8.5: Moses addressed the congregation: 'What you are about to witness is exactly what the LORD has commanded.'
8.6: Moses brought Aaron and his sons forward and bathed them with water.
8.7: He dressed Aaron in the linen tunic, tied the sash, put on the outer robe, placed the ephod over it, and secured the woven band of the ephod, fastening it all together.
8.8: He set the breastpiece over Aaron's chest and placed the Urim and Thummim inside it — the instruments of divine decision.
8.9: He placed the turban on Aaron's head and fixed the golden plate — the holy diadem — to its front, exactly as the LORD had commanded.
8.10: Moses took the anointing oil and anointed the tabernacle and all its furnishings, setting them apart as holy.
8.11: He sprinkled oil on the altar seven times, then anointed the altar with all its utensils and the bronze basin with its base — setting each one apart.
8.12: He poured the anointing oil over Aaron's head, consecrating him for his office.
8.13: Moses clothed Aaron's sons in their tunics, tied their sashes, and placed caps on their heads — all exactly as the LORD had commanded.
8.14: He brought the sin-offering bull forward, and Aaron and his sons pressed their hands against its head.
8.15: Moses slaughtered the bull and put blood on every horn of the altar with his finger, purifying it. He poured the remaining blood at the altar's base — cleansing and consecrating it for atonement.
8.16: He removed all the fat from the entrails, the liver lobe, and the kidneys with their fat, and burned them on the altar.
8.17: The rest of the bull — hide, flesh, and dung — was burned outside the camp, as the LORD had commanded.
8.18: Then he brought the ram for the burnt offering. Aaron and his sons laid their hands on its head.
8.19: Moses slaughtered it and dashed the blood against every side of the altar.
8.20: He cut the ram into pieces and burned the head, the pieces, and the fat.
8.21: He washed the entrails and legs, then burned the entire ram on the altar — a whole burnt offering, a pleasing aroma to the LORD, done exactly as commanded.
8.22: He brought the second ram — the ordination ram. Aaron and his sons laid their hands on its head.
8.23: Moses slaughtered it and daubed blood on the lobe of Aaron's right ear, the thumb of his right hand, and the big toe of his right foot — ear to hear, hand to act, foot to walk in holiness.
8.24: He did the same for Aaron's sons — blood on the right ear, right thumb, and right big toe — then dashed the remaining blood against every side of the altar.
8.25: He set aside the fat, the fat tail, all the internal fat, the liver lobe, the kidneys with their fat, and the right thigh —
8.26: and from the unleavened bread basket he took one plain loaf, one oil-bread loaf, and one wafer, laying them on top of the fat and the right thigh.
8.27: He filled Aaron's hands and his sons' hands with all of it and waved the whole bundle before the LORD.
8.28: Moses took everything from their hands and burned it on the altar with the burnt offering — an ordination sacrifice of pleasing aroma to the LORD.
8.29: Moses took the breast for himself and waved it before the LORD — his own portion from the ordination ram, as the LORD had commanded.
8.30: Moses took the anointing oil mixed with altar blood and sprinkled it over Aaron and his sons, over their vestments — consecrating them all together, priest and clothing alike.
8.31: Moses told Aaron and his sons: 'Cook the meat at the tent of meeting entrance and eat it there with the bread from the ordination basket. This is what was commanded — Aaron and his sons eat it.'
8.32: Whatever meat or bread is left over must be burned.
8.33: Do not leave the tent of meeting entrance for seven days — the ordination takes the full seven days to complete.
8.34: Everything done today was commanded by the LORD to cover your sins and consecrate you for service.
8.35: Stay at the tent of meeting entrance day and night for seven days, keeping the watch the LORD has set. Your lives depend on it — I am under the same command.
8.36: Aaron and his sons did everything the LORD had commanded through Moses — without exception.

9.1: On the eighth day — the first day after the ordination week — Moses summoned Aaron and his sons along with the elders of Israel.
9.2: He told Aaron: 'Take a flawless bull calf as your sin offering and a flawless ram as a burnt offering, and present them before the LORD.'
9.3: Tell Israel to bring: a male goat for a sin offering, and a yearling calf and a yearling lamb — all without blemish — for a burnt offering,
9.4: and an ox and a ram for fellowship offerings before the LORD, with a grain offering mixed with oil. Today the LORD himself will appear to you.
9.5: Everything Moses commanded was brought before the tent of meeting. The whole congregation assembled and stood before the LORD.
9.6: Moses announced: 'This is what the LORD commanded. Do it — and the glory of the LORD will appear among you.'
9.7: Moses told Aaron: 'Step forward to the altar. Offer your own sin offering and burnt offering first — atone for yourself. Then bring the people's offerings and atone for them. This is what the LORD commanded.'
9.8: Aaron stepped up to the altar and slaughtered the sin-offering calf — his own offering first.
9.9: His sons handed him the blood. Aaron dipped his finger and marked every horn of the altar, then poured the remaining blood at its base.
9.10: The fat, kidneys, and liver lobe he burned on the altar — exactly as the LORD had commanded Moses.
9.11: The flesh and hide he burned outside the camp.
9.12: He slaughtered the burnt offering. His sons handed him the blood, and he dashed it against every side of the altar.
9.13: His sons handed him the pieces and the head, and he burned them all on the altar.
9.14: He washed the entrails and legs, then burned them with the rest of the burnt offering.
9.15: Next he handled the people's offerings. The sin-offering goat was slaughtered and offered for the people's sin, following the same rite as his own.
9.16: He presented the burnt offering according to the established procedure.
9.17: He presented the grain offering, burned a symbolic handful on the altar — adding this to the morning burnt offering already on the fire.
9.18: He slaughtered the ox and the ram — the people's fellowship offerings. His sons handed him the blood, which he dashed against every side of the altar.
9.19: The fat portions from both animals — the fat tail, the internal fat layer, the kidneys, the liver lobe —
9.20: were laid on the breasts, then Aaron burned the fat on the altar.
9.21: The breasts and the right thigh Aaron lifted and waved before the LORD — the wave offering, as Moses had directed.
9.22: Aaron turned toward the people, raised his hands, and blessed them. Then he came down from the altar, having completed the sin offering, burnt offering, and fellowship offerings.
9.23: Moses and Aaron went together into the tent of meeting. When they came out, they blessed the people — and the glory of the LORD blazed into view before the entire assembly.
9.24: Fire burst out from the presence of the LORD and devoured the burnt offering and the fat on the altar. When the people saw it, they erupted in a shout — and fell on their faces before God.

10.1: Then Nadab and Abihu, Aaron's sons, each grabbed his incense censer, loaded it with fire and incense, and offered unholy fire before the LORD — something he had never commanded.
10.2: Fire came out from the presence of the LORD and consumed them. They died before the LORD.
10.3: Moses said to Aaron: 'This is what the LORD meant: those who draw near to me must treat me as holy — and before all the people I must be seen as glorious.' Aaron said nothing.
10.4: Moses summoned Mishael and Elzaphan — cousins of Aaron through Uzziel — and told them: 'Come and carry your kinsmen away from the sanctuary and out of the camp.'
10.5: They came and carried the two bodies — still in their priestly tunics — out of the camp, as Moses had said.
10.6: Moses told Aaron and his remaining sons Eleazar and Ithamar: 'Do not dishevel your hair or tear your clothes in mourning — that would bring death on you and God's anger on the whole congregation. Let the rest of Israel mourn this fire the LORD has kindled. But not you.'
10.7: Do not leave the tent of meeting entrance — leaving would bring death on you, for you bear the LORD's anointing oil. They obeyed.
10.8: The LORD spoke directly to Aaron:
10.9: You and your sons are forbidden to drink wine or strong drink before entering the tent of meeting — it is a death sentence if you do. This is a permanent law for every generation of priests.
10.10: You must be able to tell the difference between what is holy and what is common, between what is impure and what is clean —
10.11: and you must teach Israel all the statutes the LORD spoke through Moses. A priest who cannot discern cannot teach.
10.12: Moses told Aaron and his two remaining sons: 'Take the leftover grain offering from the LORD's fire offerings and eat it unleavened beside the altar — it is most holy.'
10.13: Eat it in the sacred precinct — this is your portion and your sons' portion from the LORD's offerings. This is what I was commanded.
10.14: The wave breast and the raised thigh may be eaten in any ritually clean place — you, your sons, your daughters together — for these are the family's portion from Israel's fellowship offerings.
10.15: The raised thigh and the wave breast are brought with the fat offerings and waved before the LORD — then given permanently to you and your descendants. The LORD has commanded it.
10.16: Moses then looked for the sin-offering goat — and found it had been burned entirely. He was furious with Eleazar and Ithamar and demanded:
10.17: 'Why did you not eat the sin offering in the sanctuary? It is most holy. God gave it to you for exactly this — to bear the congregation's guilt and atone for them before the LORD. You were supposed to eat it.'
10.18: 'Since its blood was not brought into the inner sanctuary, you were required to eat it in the holy place. That was the command.'
10.19: Aaron answered Moses quietly: 'Today my sons offered their sin offering and burnt offering before the LORD. And look at what has happened to me. Could I have eaten the sin offering today — in this grief — and would the LORD have found it acceptable?'
10.20: When Moses heard Aaron's answer, he accepted it.

11.1: The LORD spoke to Moses and Aaron together:
11.2: Tell the people of Israel which land animals they may eat.
11.3: Any land animal that has a fully split hoof and chews its cud may be eaten.
11.4: But these fail one of the two tests. The camel chews the cud but its hoof is not split — it is impure for you.
11.5: The rock badger chews the cud but has no split hoof — it is impure for you.
11.6: The hare chews the cud but has no split hoof — it is impure for you.
11.7: The pig has a fully split hoof but does not chew its cud — it is impure for you.
11.8: Do not eat their meat. Do not even touch their carcasses — they are impure to you.
11.9: In the water — sea or river — eat whatever has both fins and scales.
11.10: Anything in the water without fins and scales — every swarming sea creature, every other water-dweller — is detestable to you.
11.11: They are to remain detestable — do not eat their flesh, and treat their carcasses as something repugnant.
11.13: Among birds, these are detestable — do not eat them: the eagle, the bearded vulture, the black vulture,
11.14: the red kite, the black kite — any of their kind,
11.16: the horned owl, the screech owl, the sea gull, every kind of hawk,
11.17: the little owl, the cormorant, the short-eared owl,
11.18: the barn owl, the tawny owl, the carrion vulture,
11.19: the stork, every kind of heron, the hoopoe, and the bat.
11.20: All winged insects that walk on four legs are detestable.
11.21: However, some winged insects that walk on four legs you may eat — specifically those with jointed hind legs for leaping:
11.22: any kind of locust, any kind of migratory locust, any kind of cricket, any kind of grasshopper — all of these may be eaten.
11.23: All other four-legged winged insects are detestable.
11.24: Contact with their carcasses makes you impure until evening.
11.25: Whoever carries any part of their carcass must wash his clothes and remains impure until evening.
11.26: Any animal that divides the hoof partially but not fully, or that does not chew its cud — touching its carcass makes a person impure.
11.27: Among four-footed animals, any that walk on paws — cats, dogs, lions — are impure to you. Touch their carcasses: impure until evening.
11.28: Carry their carcasses: wash your clothes, remain impure until evening.
11.29: Among ground-crawling creatures, these are impure to you: the weasel, the mouse, the great lizard in any variety,
11.30: the gecko, the monitor lizard, the wall lizard, the skink, and the chameleon.
11.31: These are impure to you among all swarming things. Touch a dead one: impure until evening.
11.32: If a dead one falls on any object — wooden utensil, garment, leather item, sack — that object must be soaked in water. It remains impure until evening, then it is clean.
11.33: If one falls into a clay pot, everything inside it is impure — and the pot itself must be smashed.
11.34: Any edible food that water from such a vessel touches becomes impure. Any drinkable liquid from such a vessel is impure.
11.35: If any part of their carcass falls on an oven or cooking stove, it must be demolished — such things become permanently impure.
11.36: A natural spring or a cistern remains clean even if a carcass falls into it — but whoever touches the carcass in the water becomes impure.
11.37: If a carcass falls on dry seed grain set aside for planting, the seed remains clean.
11.38: But if the seed has been wet and a carcass falls on it, it becomes impure.
11.39: Even a clean animal that dies naturally — touch its carcass and you are impure until evening.
11.40: Eat from its carcass: wash your clothes, impure until evening. Carry its carcass: wash your clothes, impure until evening.
11.41: Every ground-swarming creature is detestable — none may be eaten.
11.42: Anything that slithers on its belly, walks on four legs, or has many legs — any ground-swarming creature — is detestable and may not be eaten.
11.43: Do not make yourselves detestable by eating any swarming thing. Do not defile yourselves through them.
11.44: For I am the LORD your God. Set yourselves apart — be holy — because I am holy. Do not defile yourselves with any ground-crawling creature.
11.45: I am the LORD who brought you up out of Egypt to be your God. I am holy — therefore you must be holy.
11.46: This is the comprehensive ruling covering land animals, birds, every creature that moves through water, and every swarming ground creature —
11.47: establishing the difference between the impure and the pure, between what Israel may eat and what they must not touch.

12.2: Tell Israel: when a woman bears a son, she is impure for seven days — the same length as her menstrual impurity.
12.3: On the eighth day the boy shall be circumcised.
12.4: After those seven days she enters a thirty-three-day purification period. During this time she may not touch anything consecrated or enter the sanctuary — until the full purification is complete.
12.5: If she bears a daughter, the initial impurity is two weeks — double — and the subsequent purification period is sixty-six days.
12.6: When the purification period ends — whether for a son or a daughter — she brings two offerings to the priest at the tent of meeting: a yearling lamb as a burnt offering and a young pigeon or turtledove as a sin offering.
12.7: The priest presents both before the LORD and makes atonement for her. She is then ritually clean from her blood discharge. This is the ruling for any mother, whether her child is a son or a daughter.
12.8: If a lamb is beyond her means, two turtledoves or two young pigeons serve the same purpose — one for the burnt offering, one for the sin offering. The priest makes atonement, and she is clean. No mother is barred from purification by poverty.

13.1: The LORD spoke to Moses and Aaron together:
13.2: If anyone develops a swelling, an eruption, or a gleaming patch on the skin of the body that looks like a defiling skin disease, he must be brought to Aaron the priest or one of his priestly sons.
13.3: The priest examines the mark on the skin. If the hair in the affected area has turned white and the lesion appears to go deeper than the surrounding skin, it is a defiling skin disease — the priest pronounces him impure.
13.4: If the gleaming patch is white but not visibly deeper than the skin, and the hair in it has not turned white, the priest places the person under a seven-day watch.
13.5: On the seventh day the priest examines him again. If the mark looks the same and has not spread, he extends the isolation another seven days.
13.6: At the end of the second seven days, if the mark has faded and not spread, the priest declares him clean — it was only an eruption. He washes his clothes and is clean.
13.7: But if the eruption has spread considerably after the priest examined him for his cleansing, he must return for another examination.
13.8: If the priest sees that the eruption has spread, he pronounces the person impure — it is a defiling skin disease.
13.9: Whenever someone has a defiling skin disease, he is brought to the priest.
13.10: The priest examines him. If the swelling is white, the hair in it has turned white, and raw flesh is visible in the swelling —
13.11: this is a long-established skin disease. The priest pronounces him impure without quarantine — the disease is clearly established.
13.12: But if the disease breaks out and spreads to cover the entire body — from head to foot, wherever the priest looks —
13.13: the priest makes his assessment: if the disease has covered the entire body and turned it white, he pronounces the person clean. Complete whitening, paradoxically, signals cleanliness.
13.14: The moment raw flesh appears, however, he is impure.
13.15: The priest sees the raw flesh and pronounces him impure — raw flesh is a mark of active disease.
13.16: If the raw flesh later turns white, he returns to the priest.
13.17: The priest examines him. If the whole affected area has turned white, the priest pronounces him clean.
13.18: When a boil has appeared on the body and healed,
13.19: but a white swelling or a reddish-white gleaming patch appears in the healed spot, the person shows it to the priest.
13.20: If the priest sees it is deeper than the surrounding skin and the hair in it has turned white, he pronounces the person impure — a skin disease has erupted at the site of the old boil.
13.21: But if the priest finds no white hair in it, it is no deeper than the skin, and it has faded, the person is quarantined seven days.
13.22: If it spreads, the priest pronounces him impure — it is a diseased mark.
13.23: If the gleaming patch stays put and has not spread, it is simply the scar of the healed boil — the priest pronounces the person clean.
13.24: When someone has been burned and the scar develops a reddish-white or white gleaming patch in the healing flesh,
13.25: the priest examines it. If the hair in the patch has turned white and the lesion appears deeper than the skin, a defiling skin disease has erupted in the burn wound — the priest pronounces him impure.
13.26: But if there is no white hair in it, it is no deeper than the skin, and it has faded — seven days of quarantine.
13.27: On the seventh day, if the patch has spread significantly, the priest pronounces impurity — it is a skin disease.
13.28: But if the patch has stayed in place, has not spread, and has faded, it is the residual swelling from the burn — not a disease. The priest pronounces the person clean.
13.29: When anyone — man or woman — develops a mark on the scalp or chin area,
13.30: the priest examines it. If it appears deeper than the skin and contains thin, yellowish hair, the priest pronounces impurity — it is a scalp itch, a skin disease of the head or chin area.
13.31: But if the itch appears no deeper than the skin and contains no black hair, the person is placed under seven-day quarantine.
13.32: On the seventh day, if the itch has not spread, still shows no yellow hair, and is no deeper than the skin —
13.33: the person shaves all hair except the affected patch, and the priest extends the quarantine another seven days.
13.34: On the seventh day, if the itch has not spread and remains no deeper than the skin, the priest pronounces him clean. He washes his clothes and is clean.
13.35: If the itch spreads significantly after the priest has declared cleanliness —
13.36: the priest examines him again. If it has spread, the yellow-hair test is unnecessary — the spread itself establishes impurity.
13.37: But if the itch appears stable and black hair has grown back in it, the itch is healed — the priest pronounces him clean.
13.38: When a man or woman has white gleaming patches on the skin of the body,
13.39: the priest examines them. If the patches are dull, faded white, it is a harmless surface eruption — the person is clean.
13.40: Baldness from the top of the head is not a disease — the man is clean.
13.41: Frontal baldness likewise makes no one impure — clean.
13.42: But if a reddish-white mark appears on the bald scalp or bald forehead, a defiling skin disease has broken out there.
13.43: The priest examines it. If the swelling on the bald area is reddish-white and looks the same as a skin disease on the body —
13.44: the man has a defiling skin disease and is impure. The priest pronounces him unclean — the affliction is on his head.
13.45: The person declared impure must wear torn clothing, keep his hair disheveled, cover his mouth, and call out wherever he goes: 'Unclean! Unclean!' — the outward signs of ritual exclusion.
13.46: For as long as the disease is active, he is impure. He lives alone, outside the camp — separated from the covenant community until the disease is healed.
13.47: The priestly category of defiling skin disease extends to garments — wool or linen:
13.48: in the warp threads or the woof threads of linen or wool, or in leather goods of any kind —
13.49: if the mark is greenish or reddish — on garment, leather, warp, or woof — it is a diseased mark and must be brought to the priest.
13.50: The priest examines it and quarantines the afflicted article for seven days.
13.51: On the seventh day, if the mark has spread through the fabric or leather, it is a destructive disease — the article is impure.
13.52: Burn it — garment, warp, woof, wool, linen, or leather — whatever carries the destructive mark. Fire, not quarantine.
13.53: But if the priest finds the mark has not spread in the garment, warp, woof, or leather —
13.54: he orders the afflicted article washed and imposes another seven days of quarantine.
13.55: After washing, if the mark has not faded and has not spread, it is impure — burn it. Whether the discoloration is inside or outside the weave, it is a deep-seated stain.
13.56: If after washing the mark has visibly faded, the priest tears that section out of the garment or leather.
13.57: If the mark keeps reappearing in the garment, warp, woof, or leather, it is spreading — burn that article.
13.58: A garment, warp, woof, or leather article from which the mark has fully departed after washing shall be washed again and declared clean.
13.59: This is the comprehensive ruling for diagnosing a defiling mark in wool or linen garments, warp or woof threads, or any leather goods — determining what is clean and what is impure.

14.2: This is the procedure for the day when someone is declared clean from a skin disease — the person is brought to the priest.
14.3: The priest goes outside the camp to examine him. If the skin disease has indeed healed —
14.4: the priest orders four things for the cleansing rite: two living clean birds, cedar wood, crimson yarn, and hyssop.
14.5: One bird is slaughtered over fresh water in a clay pot — the blood mingles with living water.
14.6: The living bird, cedar wood, crimson yarn, and hyssop are all dipped together in the blood-and-water mixture — binding life to the symbols of purification.
14.7: The priest sprinkles the person seven times, pronounces him clean, then releases the living bird into the open field — the bird carries the disease away.
14.8: The cleansed person washes clothes, shaves all hair, and bathes — then he is clean and may enter the camp. But he waits outside his own tent for seven more days, easing back into community life.
14.9: On the seventh day he shaves completely — head, beard, eyebrows, every hair — washes his clothes, bathes. Full cleansing before reintegration.
14.10: On the eighth day the full offering sequence begins: two flawless male lambs, one flawless yearling ewe, three-tenths of an ephah of fine flour mixed with oil, and one log of oil.
14.11: The priest presents the man being cleansed with all his offerings before the LORD at the tent of meeting entrance.
14.12: The priest takes one lamb as a reparation offering and the log of oil and waves them before the LORD — the guilt offering comes first.
14.13: The guilt-offering lamb is slaughtered in the sacred precinct, as for the sin offering and burnt offering. Like the sin offering, it belongs to the officiating priest — it is most holy.
14.14: The priest takes blood from the reparation offering and touches it to three points: the right earlobe, the right thumb, the right big toe — ear to hear, hand to act, foot to walk in clean ways.
14.15: The priest pours oil from the log into his left palm.
14.16: With his right finger he sprinkles oil seven times before the LORD.
14.17: The remaining oil goes to the same three spots where the blood was placed — right earlobe, right thumb, right big toe — oil layered over blood.
14.18: The priest pours what remains on the head of the one being cleansed, then makes atonement for him before the LORD.
14.19: The sin offering is presented next — atonement for the uncleanness itself — then the burnt offering follows.
14.20: Burnt offering and grain offering complete the sequence on the altar. The priest makes atonement and the person is clean — fully restored to the community.
14.21: For someone who cannot afford the full offering: one lamb as a reparation offering, one-tenth ephah of fine flour mixed with oil, and a log of oil —
14.22: plus two turtledoves or two young pigeons — one sin offering, one burnt offering. What he can afford is enough.
14.23: On the eighth day he brings these to the priest at the tent of meeting entrance before the LORD.
14.24: The priest waves the guilt-offering lamb and the oil before the LORD.
14.25: The guilt-offering lamb is slaughtered. Blood touches the right earlobe, right thumb, right big toe of the person being cleansed.
14.26: The priest pours oil into his left palm,
14.27: and sprinkles oil seven times before the LORD with his right finger.
14.28: Oil is placed on the same three points where the blood was — right earlobe, right thumb, right big toe.
14.29: The remaining oil is poured on the head of the one being cleansed — atonement made before the LORD.
14.30: Then comes whichever bird offering the person can afford —
14.31: one for a sin offering, one for a burnt offering, with the grain offering. The priest makes atonement before the LORD — poverty is no barrier to cleansing.
14.32: This is the provision for anyone with a skin disease who lacks the means for the standard offering.
14.33: The LORD spoke to Moses and Aaron:
14.34: When you enter the land I am giving you, and a defiling growth appears on a house you occupy —
14.35: the owner reports to the priest: 'Something like a diseased mark has appeared in my house.'
14.36: The priest orders the house emptied before he enters to examine it — this prevents everything inside from becoming impure by association. Then the priest inspects.
14.37: If the marks on the walls are greenish or reddish streaks that appear to go deeper than the wall surface —
14.38: the priest steps out to the doorway and places the house under seven-day quarantine.
14.39: On the seventh day, if the marks have spread on the walls —
14.40: the priest orders the diseased stones removed and dumped outside the city in an impure place.
14.41: The entire interior is scraped down, and all the debris taken outside the city to an impure site.
14.42: New stones replace the old; new plaster covers the walls.
14.43: If the disease returns and reappears after all the work — stones removed, walls scraped, house replastered —
14.44: the priest returns for examination. If the mark has spread despite all remediation, it is a destructive disease in the house — the house is impure.
14.45: The house is demolished completely — stones, timber, plaster — and all of it carried outside the city to an impure site.
14.46: Entering the quarantined house makes a person impure until evening.
14.47: Sleep in the quarantined house: wash your clothes. Eat in it: wash your clothes.
14.48: But if the priest examines after replastering and the mark has not spread, the priest pronounces the house clean — the disease has been arrested.
14.49: The same four elements used for personal cleansing serve the house: two birds, cedar wood, crimson yarn, hyssop.
14.50: One bird is slaughtered over fresh water in a clay vessel.
14.51: Cedar wood, hyssop, crimson yarn, and living bird are all dipped in blood-and-water and used to sprinkle the house seven times.
14.52: The house is purified by the blood, the living water, the living bird, the cedar, the hyssop, the crimson — a full-spectrum purification rite.
14.53: The living bird is released outside the city into open country — carrying the defilement away. Atonement is made for the house; it is clean.
14.54: This is the comprehensive ruling for every form of defiling skin disease — for an itch,
14.55: for disease in clothing and in buildings,
14.56: for swelling, eruption, and gleaming patch —
14.57: establishing which conditions are impure and which are clean. This is the full ruling on defiling skin disease.

15.1: The LORD spoke to Moses and Aaron:
15.2: Tell Israel: when a man has an abnormal bodily discharge, the discharge renders him impure.
15.3: Whether the discharge flows freely or is intermittent, he is impure — the condition itself creates the impurity.
15.4: Every surface he lies on becomes impure; every surface he sits on becomes impure.
15.5: Touch his bed: wash clothes, bathe, impure until evening.
15.6: Sit on what he sat on: wash clothes, bathe, impure until evening.
15.7: Touch his body: wash clothes, bathe, impure until evening.
15.8: Even his saliva transfers impurity — anyone he spits on washes clothes, bathes, remains impure until evening.
15.9: Any saddle he rides on is impure.
15.10: Touch anything that was under him: impure until evening. Carry it: wash clothes, bathe, impure until evening.
15.11: If he touches someone without rinsing his hands first, that person washes clothes, bathes, remains impure until evening.
15.12: Clay vessels he touches must be smashed; wooden ones rinsed with water.
15.13: When the discharge stops, he counts seven days, washes his clothes, bathes in running water — then he is clean.
15.14: On the eighth day he brings two turtledoves or two young pigeons to the priest at the tent of meeting entrance.
15.15: The priest offers one as a sin offering and one as a burnt offering, making atonement before the LORD for the discharge — restoration complete.
15.16: A man who has a seminal emission bathes his entire body and is impure until evening.
15.17: Any garment or leather that has the emission on it is washed — impure until evening.
15.18: Sexual intercourse requires both partners to bathe afterward — both are impure until evening.
15.19: When a woman's discharge is blood — her monthly cycle — she is in menstrual separation for seven days. Anyone who touches her is impure until evening.
15.20: Everything she lies on and everything she sits on during her separation is impure.
15.21: Touch her bed: wash clothes, bathe, impure until evening.
15.22: Touch anything she sat on: wash clothes, bathe, impure until evening.
15.23: Bed or seat — touching either makes the person impure until evening.
15.24: If a man has sexual relations with her and her menstrual impurity transfers to him, he becomes impure for seven full days — and every bed he uses during that time is impure.
15.25: If a woman's blood discharge occurs outside her regular cycle or lasts beyond it, she is impure for the duration of that discharge — the same rules apply as for her monthly separation.
15.26: Every bed and every seat she uses during the extended discharge carries the same impurity as during her regular monthly separation.
15.27: Anyone who touches these surfaces washes clothes, bathes, remains impure until evening.
15.28: When the discharge stops, she counts seven days — then she is clean.
15.29: On the eighth day she brings two turtledoves or two young pigeons to the priest at the tent of meeting entrance.
15.30: Sin offering and burnt offering — the priest makes atonement before the LORD for her. The extended discharge required priestly restoration, not just the passage of time.
15.31: This is why Israel must be kept separate from impurity: the tabernacle dwells in their midst. Impurity that reaches the sanctuary brings death — the entire purity system exists to protect the dwelling of the holy God among an unholy people.
15.32: This is the ruling for the man with an abnormal discharge and for the man with a seminal emission,
15.33: for the woman in her menstrual separation, for anyone with an extended discharge, and for the man who has relations with a woman in her impurity.

16.1: The LORD spoke to Moses in the aftermath of Nadab and Abihu's death — they who died when they drew near the LORD without authorization.
16.2: Tell Aaron: access to the Most Holy Place is not open to him at will. To enter whenever he pleases is to die — for I appear in the cloud over the atonement cover. The divine presence is not to be approached casually.
16.3: Aaron may enter only by this prescribed path: a bull calf as his personal sin offering and a ram for a burnt offering.
16.4: He enters in plain linen — not the ornate high-priestly garments. Linen tunic, linen trousers, linen sash, linen turban — these are the simple holy garments worn into God's direct presence. He bathes first, then dresses.
16.5: From the congregation he takes two male goats for the sin offering — the two-goat drama is Israel's offering — and one ram for a burnt offering.
16.6: Aaron begins with atonement for himself and his household — the priest must be clean before he can atone for others.
16.7: Both goats are brought and presented before the LORD at the tent of meeting entrance.
16.8: Aaron casts lots to divide the two goats: one lot falls for the LORD, one for Azazel — the wilderness goat of removal.
16.9: The goat that the lot assigns to the LORD is offered as a sin offering.
16.10: The Azazel goat stays alive — it is presented before the LORD for the atonement-confession rite, then sent away into the wilderness as the goat of removal.
16.11: Aaron slaughters his bull first — personal atonement before he can mediate atonement for others.
16.12: He fills a censer with live coals from the altar before the LORD, takes two handfuls of ground fragrant incense, and carries them inside the veil — into the Most Holy Place.
16.13: He puts the incense on the coals before the LORD — the cloud of incense envelops the atonement cover. The incense cloud screens Aaron from the direct sight of God — without it, he would die.
16.14: He takes some of the bull's blood and sprinkles it on the east face of the atonement cover with his finger, then sprinkles seven times in front of the cover — blood meeting the place where God meets humanity.
16.15: He slaughters the sin-offering goat for the people and brings its blood inside the veil. The same act repeated: blood on and before the atonement cover — this time for the whole congregation.
16.16: By this rite he atones for the holy place itself — Israel's accumulated impurity and transgressions contaminate even the sanctuary. The same atonement covers the tent of meeting, which stands in the midst of an impure people.
16.17: No one else enters the tent of meeting from when Aaron goes in until he comes out. The moment demands absolute solitude — Aaron stands alone between the holy God and the whole congregation of Israel.
16.18: Coming out, he moves to the altar before the LORD and atones for that too. Mixed blood — from the bull and the goat — is applied to every horn of the altar.
16.19: Seven sprinklings of blood cleanse the altar from the accumulated impurity of Israel — consecrating it fresh for a new year of service.
16.20: With the sanctuary, the tent, and the altar all atoned for, the Azazel goat is brought forward — the second movement of Yom Kippur.
16.21: Aaron places both hands on the head of the live goat and confesses over it — naming all Israel's iniquities, transgressions, and sins. The laying on of hands transfers the full weight of the nation's guilt onto the goat, which is then led away into the wilderness by a designated man.
16.22: The goat carries Israel's sins to a desolate, uninhabited place — a land of no return. There the guilt is released, far from the community and far from God's house.
16.23: Aaron returns to the tent of meeting and removes the plain linen garments worn into the Most Holy Place. They are left there — the garments that entered God's presence are not worn again.
16.24: He bathes in the sacred precinct, dresses in his regular priestly garments, and comes out to offer the burnt offerings — his own and the people's — completing the atonement of the day.
16.25: The fat portions of the sin offerings are burned on the altar.
16.26: The man who led the goat away has carried proximity to the sin-laden animal — he washes clothes and bathes before returning to the camp.
16.27: The bull and goat whose blood was used inside the Most Holy Place are burned completely outside the camp — hides, flesh, offal — nothing kept. The sin bearer cannot be eaten.
16.28: The man who burns them also cleanses himself — wash clothes, bathe — before returning to camp.
16.29: This is a permanent statute: the tenth day of the seventh month is the Day of Atonement. Israelite and resident alien alike — fast and humble yourselves, do no work. The calendar makes Yom Kippur annual and universal within Israel's community.
16.30: On this one day, once a year, atonement is made for the whole community — every sin, cleanly dealt with before the LORD. The clean slate is the gift of Yom Kippur.
16.31: It is a Sabbath of complete, solemn rest — fast and be still. This is the permanent law.
16.32: When Aaron dies, his anointed and ordained successor will perform this rite — dressed in the same plain holy linen, entering the same way. The ritual does not depend on Aaron personally; it is built into the priestly office itself.
16.33: The scope of atonement on this day covers everything: the inner sanctuary, the tent, the altar, the priests themselves, the entire congregation. Nothing and no one is excluded.
16.34: Once a year — always and only once — complete atonement is made for all Israel's sins. Aaron did as the LORD commanded Moses. The Day of Atonement is established.

17.2: Tell Aaron, his sons, and all Israel: this is what the LORD commands —
17.3: Any Israelite who slaughters a domestic animal — ox, sheep, or goat — whether inside the camp or outside it —
17.4: but does not bring it to the tent of meeting entrance to offer before the LORD — bloodguilt is charged to that man. He has shed blood. He is cut off from his people. All legitimate slaughter must pass through the altar.
17.5: The purpose is to channel all Israel's animal offerings through the single legitimate altar. What was once slaughtered wherever becomes a fellowship offering at the tent of meeting.
17.6: The priest dashes the blood on the LORD's altar and burns the fat — a pleasing aroma. The meal becomes an act of worship.
17.7: The deeper reason: Israel has been offering to goat-demons — the spirit-beings of the wilderness. This law cuts that off permanently. All sacrifice must go to the LORD and to him alone. A permanent statute.
17.8: Say to them: any Israelite or resident alien who brings a burnt offering or other sacrifice —
17.9: but does not bring it to the tent of meeting entrance to offer to the LORD — that person is cut off from the community. No private altars; no rival shrines.
17.10: Any Israelite or resident alien who eats blood in any form — I will set my face against that person and cut them off. This is a direct divine sanction, not merely a ritual infraction.
17.11: Here is why: blood carries the life-force of the creature. I have assigned it to the altar to make atonement for human life — life ransoms life. This is the theological foundation of the entire sacrificial system.
17.12: That is why no one in Israel — and no resident alien — may eat blood. It belongs to the altar.
17.13: When an Israelite or resident alien hunts and kills a game animal or bird that is clean for eating, he pours out the blood and covers it with soil — returning the life to the earth, refusing to consume it.
17.14: The life of every creature is its blood — blood and life are one. Therefore: no blood of any creature may be eaten by anyone in Israel. Eat it and be cut off. The prohibition is grounded in the sanctity of life itself.
17.15: Anyone who eats an animal that died on its own or was killed by a predator — their blood was not properly drained — must wash clothes, bathe, and wait until evening. Then clean.
17.16: If he skips the washing and bathing, he bears the guilt himself — the cleansing is required, not optional.

18.2: Tell Israel: I am the LORD your God.
18.3: Egypt behind you, Canaan ahead — you shall not live by the customs of either. What the nations do, you shall not do. You are not shaped by where you were or where you are going; you are shaped by who I am.
18.4: Walk in my rules and keep my statutes. I am the LORD your God — that is the only identity that determines how you live.
18.5: Keep my statutes and rules. The one who does them lives by them — real life, life as God intended. I am the LORD.
18.6: No one is to have sexual relations with a close relative. I am the LORD — the prohibition has the full force of divine authority.
18.7: Do not have sexual relations with your father or your mother. She is your mother — this boundary is absolute.
18.8: Do not have sexual relations with your father's wife — even a stepmother. She belongs to your father's intimate sphere; to violate her is to violate him.
18.9: Do not have sexual relations with your sister — full sister or half-sister, raised in the same home or not. The sibling bond forecloses this.
18.10: Do not have sexual relations with your granddaughter — son's or daughter's. In harming them you harm yourself — their nakedness is an extension of your own.
18.11: The daughter your father had by his wife — your half-sister on his side — is your sister. Do not have sexual relations with her.
18.12: Do not have sexual relations with your father's sister — she is your father's own kin, and therefore yours.
18.13: Do not have sexual relations with your mother's sister — she is your mother's kin.
18.14: Do not violate your father's brother's household — do not have sexual relations with his wife. She is your aunt.
18.15: Do not have sexual relations with your daughter-in-law. She is your son's wife — that relationship belongs to him.
18.16: Do not have sexual relations with your brother's wife. She belongs to his intimate sphere — to have her is to violate him.
18.17: Do not have sexual relations with a woman and also with her daughter, or her granddaughter. They are close kin to each other; to take them together or in succession is moral corruption — it twists the bonds that define a family.
18.18: Do not marry a woman's sister as a rival while the first sister is still living. The anguish of such rivalry makes the arrangement an act of cruelty. Sisters are not competitors.
18.19: Do not have sexual relations with a woman during her menstrual impurity. Her body in its monthly cycle is not to be violated.
18.20: Do not have sexual relations with your neighbor's wife. It defiles you — and it destroys him.
18.21: Do not give any of your children as an offering to Molech. To do so is to profane the name of your God — you cannot worship Molech and call yourself the LORD's. I am the LORD.
18.22: Male-with-male sexual intercourse — this is an abomination.
18.23: Do not have sexual relations with an animal — man or woman. This is a confusion of kinds, a violation of the boundaries built into creation itself.
18.24: Do not defile yourselves through any of these acts. The nations currently occupying the land defiled themselves by all of them.
18.25: And the land became defiled. The land itself is a moral actor in this theology — I punished its iniquity, and the land vomited out those who corrupted it. This is why those nations are being displaced.
18.26: You shall keep my statutes and rules. None of these abominations — Israelite or resident alien. The moral law applies to all who live in the land.
18.27: The people before you did all these things. That is why the land became defiled.
18.28: Do not defile the land — or it will vomit you out too, just as it vomited them. Israel is not exempt from the land's moral logic.
18.29: Anyone who does these things is cut off from the community. The penalty is exclusion — severing from the covenant people.
18.30: Keep my charge: do not practice these abominations that your predecessors practiced; do not defile yourselves through them. I am the LORD your God — your identity is determined by who I am, not by what the nations do.

19.2: Address the whole congregation of Israel: Be holy — for I, the LORD your God, am holy.
19.3: Revere your father and your mother — every one of you. Keep my sabbaths. I am the LORD your God.
19.4: Do not turn toward worthless idols or cast metal images and call them gods. I am the LORD your God.
19.5: When you bring a fellowship offering to the LORD, bring it in the right spirit so it will be received.
19.6: Eat the fellowship sacrifice the day you offer it, or the next — nothing beyond. Whatever reaches the third day goes into the fire.
19.7: Eat it on the third day and it becomes offensive — the offering is invalidated, not received.
19.8: Anyone who eats it carries the guilt himself — he has profaned what is consecrated to the LORD. That person is cut off from Israel.
19.9: When you harvest your fields, do not clear the corners all the way to the edge, and do not pick up what falls behind the harvesters.
19.10: Do not strip your vineyard clean or pick up every fallen grape. Leave something for the poor and the stranger among you. I am the LORD your God.
19.11: Do not steal. Do not deceive. Do not lie to one another.
19.12: Do not invoke my name to back a lie — that profanes the name of your God. I am the LORD.
19.13: Do not cheat your neighbor or rob him. Pay a hired worker his wages the same day — do not hold it back overnight.
19.14: Do not curse the person who cannot hear your insult. Do not place an obstacle before the blind. Fear your God. I am the LORD.
19.15: In the courtroom, do not tilt the scales — neither toward the poor out of sympathy nor toward the powerful out of fear. Judge every neighbor by what is right.
19.16: Do not spread gossip through the community. Do not stand by while your neighbor's blood is shed. I am the LORD.
19.17: Do not carry hatred toward your neighbor hidden in your heart. Speak openly — rebuke him when he sins. Otherwise you share the guilt.
19.18: Do not take revenge. Do not nurse a grudge against anyone in your community. Love your neighbor as you love yourself. I am the LORD.
19.19: Keep my statutes: do not crossbreed your livestock, do not plant your field with mixed seeds, and do not wear fabric woven from two different materials.
19.20: If a man has sexual relations with a female slave who is betrothed but not yet freed, they are not executed — she was not free. But there must be a formal penalty.
19.21: The man must bring his reparation offering to the LORD at the tent of meeting entrance — a ram.
19.22: The priest makes atonement for him with the reparation ram before the LORD, covering the sin he committed. He is forgiven.
19.23: When you enter the land and plant any fruit tree, treat its fruit as off-limits for the first three years — forbidden; do not eat it.
19.24: In the fourth year the entire yield is consecrated — a gift of praise to the LORD.
19.25: Only in the fifth year may you eat the fruit freely — and by then the tree will give its full yield. I am the LORD your God.
19.26: Do not eat anything with the blood still in it. Do not practice divination or try to read omens.
19.27: Do not shave the sides of your head or trim the edges of your beard in the manner of the nations.
19.28: Do not gash your flesh in mourning rites for the dead. Do not mark your body with tattoos. I am the LORD.
19.29: Do not exploit your daughter by forcing her into prostitution — it debases her and corrupts the whole land.
19.30: Keep my sabbaths. Hold my sanctuary in reverence. I am the LORD.
19.31: Do not consult mediums or spiritists. Do not go looking for them — you would defile yourselves. I am the LORD your God.
19.32: Rise when an elderly person enters the room. Honor the aged. In doing so you honor your God. I am the LORD.
19.33: When a foreigner lives among you in your land, do not oppress him.
19.34: Treat the foreigner who lives among you as one of your own people — love him as you love yourself. You know what it is to be a stranger: you were foreigners in Egypt. I am the LORD your God.
19.35: Do not cheat in any measurement — not in length, weight, or volume.
19.36: Use accurate scales, honest weights, a correct ephah, a correct hin — in every transaction. I am the LORD your God, who brought you out of Egypt.
19.37: Keep every statute and carry out every ruling I have given. I am the LORD.

20.2: Tell Israel: any person — Israelite or resident foreigner — who sacrifices a child to Molech shall be executed by the community with stones.
20.3: I myself will turn against that man and cut him off from his people — he has given his child to Molech, defiling my sanctuary and profaning my holy name.
20.4: If the community deliberately ignores what he is doing — watches him sacrifice his child to Molech and does nothing —
20.5: then I will turn against that man and his whole household — and against all who follow him in this faithless pursuit of Molech. I will cut them all off from Israel.
20.6: Anyone who turns to mediums and spiritists — chasing after them — I will set my face against that person and cut them off from Israel.
20.7: Therefore consecrate yourselves and be holy — I am the LORD your God.
20.8: Keep my statutes and carry them out. I am the LORD who sets you apart.
20.9: Whoever curses his father or his mother shall be put to death. He has cursed his own parents — the blood-guilt is his alone.
20.10: A man who commits adultery with another man's wife — both the man and the woman shall be put to death.
20.11: A man who lies with his father's wife has violated his father's honor — both shall be put to death; the blood-guilt is theirs.
20.12: A man who lies with his daughter-in-law — both shall be put to death. They have committed depravity; the blood-guilt is theirs.
20.13: If a man lies with a man as with a woman, both have committed what the law names an abomination. Both shall be put to death; the blood-guilt is theirs.
20.14: If a man takes both a woman and her mother as wives, it is wickedness — all three shall be burned. Such depravity must not take root among you.
20.15: A man who has sexual relations with an animal shall be put to death — and the animal killed as well.
20.16: If a woman presents herself to an animal for sexual contact, both the woman and the animal shall be put to death; the blood-guilt is theirs.
20.17: If a man has sexual relations with his sister — born of his father or his mother — they are cut off before the community. He has dishonored his sister and must bear his guilt.
20.18: If a man lies with a woman during her menstrual period, both have exposed what was to remain private — both are cut off from Israel.
20.19: Do not have sexual relations with your mother's sister or your father's sister — they are close kin. Both parties bear the guilt.
20.20: A man who lies with his uncle's wife dishonors his uncle. Both carry the guilt — and they will die childless.
20.21: Taking one's brother's wife is an indecency — he has dishonored his brother. They will be childless.
20.22: Keep every statute and carry out every ruling — or the land I am bringing you into will vomit you out, as it vomited out the nations before you.
20.23: Do not live by the practices of the nations I am expelling before you. They did all these things — that is why I have come to detest them.
20.24: I have told you: their land will be yours — a land flowing with milk and honey. I am the LORD your God who has separated you from all the nations of the earth.
20.25: Keep the distinction I have given you between clean and unclean animals, between clean and unclean birds. Do not make yourselves detestable by eating what I have set apart from you as impure.
20.26: Be holy to me — for I the LORD am holy. I have set you apart from all other peoples so that you would be mine.
20.27: Any man or woman who practices as a medium or spiritist shall be put to death by stoning. The blood-guilt is their own.

21.1: The LORD told Moses to speak to the priests — Aaron's sons — with this word: a priest must not make himself ritually impure by contact with the dead among his people,
21.2: except for his nearest blood relatives: mother, father, son, daughter, brother,
21.3: and for an unmarried sister still in his household — for her he may become impure.
21.4: As a man who holds sacred office, he must not defile himself in any way that would dishonor that office.
21.5: Priests may not shave their heads, trim the edges of their beards, or gash their bodies — the mourning rites of the nations are forbidden to them.
21.6: They are consecrated to their God and must not profane his name — they present the LORD's fire offerings, the food of their God. Therefore they must be holy.
21.7: A priest may not marry a woman who has been a prostitute or one of degraded status, nor a divorced woman — for the priest is holy to his God.
21.8: Honor the priest as holy, for he presents the food of your God. He must be holy to you — I, the LORD who sets you apart, am holy.
21.9: If a priest's daughter turns to prostitution, she dishonors her father and his sacred office — she shall be burned.
21.10: The high priest — anointed with oil and ordained to wear the sacred vestments — must never dishevel his hair in mourning or tear his garments.
21.11: He may not come near any dead body — not even for his father, not even for his mother.
21.12: He must not leave the sanctuary or dishonor the sanctuary of his God — the anointing oil marks him as wholly set apart. I am the LORD.
21.13: The high priest must marry a virgin.
21.14: He may not marry a widow, a divorcée, a woman of degraded status, or a prostitute. His wife must be a virgin from among his own people.
21.15: He must not compromise the holiness of his descendants among the people — I the LORD am the one who consecrates him.
21.17: Tell Aaron: none of his descendants, in any generation, who has a physical defect may come forward to present the food of his God.
21.18: None may come near who has a blemish — whether blind or lame, disfigured in face, or with any malformation,
21.19: with a broken foot or broken hand,
21.20: hunchbacked or dwarfed, with diseased eyes, a skin condition, scabs, or damaged reproductive organs.
21.21: Any descendant of Aaron with such a defect may not come forward to offer the LORD's fire offerings. He has a blemish and may not present the food of his God.
21.22: He is still a priest and may eat from the holy food — both the most holy portions and the ordinary sacred portions.
21.23: But he may not enter behind the veil or approach the altar — his blemish would profane the sacred space. I am the LORD who consecrates these places.
21.24: Moses conveyed all this to Aaron, to his sons, and to the whole community of Israel.

22.2: Tell Aaron and his sons: they must handle Israel's holy offerings with the greatest care, or they will profane my holy name. I am the LORD.
22.3: Tell them: any of their descendants who approaches Israel's holy offerings while ritually impure shall be cut off from my presence. I am the LORD.
22.4: Any of Aaron's descendants who has a skin disease or a bodily discharge may not eat the holy portions until he is clean. Anyone who has touched a corpse-contaminated object or has had a seminal emission,
22.5: or who has touched any creature that makes him impure, or any person in a state of impurity — whatever form the impurity takes —
22.6: that person is impure until evening and may not eat from the holy portions unless he has bathed. There are no shortcuts to ritual cleansing.
22.7: When the sun goes down he is clean — then and only then may he eat the holy food, for it is his appointed provision.
22.8: He must not eat any animal that died on its own or was killed by a wild animal — that would defile him. I am the LORD.
22.9: Let them keep my charge — if they profane it they will bear sin and die for it. I the LORD am the one who sanctifies them.
22.10: No outsider may eat the holy portions — not even a priest's resident guest or his hired worker.
22.11: If a priest purchases a slave, that slave becomes part of the household and may eat the holy food — as may anyone born in the priest's house.
