22.12: If a priest's daughter marries someone outside the priestly line, she loses access to the holy offerings.
22.13: But if a priest's daughter is widowed or divorced and childless and returns to her father's home, she may once again eat from the holy portions. No layperson may eat them, though.
22.14: If someone accidentally eats a holy portion, he must repay its full value plus a twenty-percent penalty, handing it over to the priest.
22.15: The priests must ensure that Israel's holy offerings to the LORD are not profaned —
22.16: otherwise those who eat improperly will carry guilt. I the LORD am the one who sanctifies these offerings.
22.18: Tell Aaron and his sons and all Israel: whether an Israelite or a foreigner living among you brings a burnt offering — as a vow or as a freewill gift —
22.19: it must be a flawless male — from cattle, sheep, or goats — for it to be accepted.
22.20: Anything with a defect will not be accepted — do not offer it.
22.21: Anyone who offers a fellowship sacrifice to the LORD — whether fulfilling a vow or bringing a spontaneous gift from the herd or flock — must bring what is perfect. No blemish is acceptable.
22.22: Do not bring to the LORD any animal that is blind, injured, maimed, oozing, diseased, or scabbed. None of these may go on the altar as a fire offering.
22.23: An animal that is overdeveloped or stunted in a limb may be offered as a spontaneous freewill gift — but not to fulfill a vow.
22.24: Any animal with mutilated, crushed, or surgically altered reproductive organs may not be offered to the LORD anywhere in your land.
22.25: Do not accept animals with these defects even from a foreigner who wishes to offer — their blemishes disqualify them regardless of who presents them.
22.27: A calf, lamb, or kid must stay with its mother for seven days. From the eighth day on it may be offered to the LORD.
22.28: Do not slaughter an animal and its offspring on the same day — cow and calf, ewe and lamb.
22.29: When you bring a thanksgiving sacrifice to the LORD, bring it willingly and in the right spirit so it will be received.
22.30: Eat it the same day — nothing left until morning. I am the LORD.
22.31: Keep my commandments and carry them out. I am the LORD.
22.32: Do not profane my holy name. I will be treated as holy in the midst of Israel — I am the LORD who sets you apart,
22.33: who brought you out of Egypt to be your God. I am the LORD.

23.2: Tell the Israelites: These are the LORD's appointed times, which you shall declare as sacred assemblies. They belong to me.
23.3: Work may be done on six days, but the seventh is the Sabbath — a day of complete rest, a sacred assembly. Do no work on it. It is the LORD's Sabbath wherever you live.
23.4: These are the LORD's appointed times — sacred assemblies to be proclaimed at their set seasons.
23.5: The fourteenth day of the first month at dusk: the LORD's Passover.
23.6: The fifteenth day of that same month begins the Festival of Unleavened Bread to the LORD — seven days of eating bread without yeast.
23.7: On the first day there is a sacred assembly — do no regular work.
23.8: Present fire offerings to the LORD each of the seven days. On the seventh day, again, a sacred assembly — do no ordinary work.
23.10: Tell Israel: when you enter the land I am giving you and begin harvesting, bring the first sheaf of your harvest to the priest.
23.11: The priest waves it before the LORD so that it is accepted for you. This takes place on the day after the Sabbath.
23.12: On the day of the wave offering, also bring a flawless yearling lamb as a burnt offering to the LORD.
23.13: Accompanying it: a grain offering of two-tenths of an ephah of fine flour mixed with oil, a fire offering of pleasing aroma to the LORD, and a drink offering of a quarter of a hin of wine.
23.14: Eat no bread from the new harvest, no roasted grain, no fresh ears — until the day you bring this offering to your God. This is a permanent law in every generation, wherever you live.
23.15: Starting from the day after the Sabbath when you presented the wave sheaf, count out seven full weeks —
23.16: fifty days in all, bringing you to the day after the seventh Sabbath. On that fiftieth day, present a new grain offering to the LORD.
23.17: Bring two leavened loaves from your homes — each of two-tenths of an ephah of fine flour — as a wave offering to the LORD. These are the firstfruits.
23.18: Along with the bread, offer seven flawless yearling lambs, one young bull, and two rams — all as burnt offerings to the LORD, with their grain and drink offerings. A pleasing aroma to the LORD.
23.19: Also sacrifice a male goat as a sin offering and two yearling lambs as fellowship offerings.
23.20: The priest waves the two lambs with the firstfruits bread before the LORD. These belong to the LORD — and go to the priest.
23.21: Declare that same day a sacred assembly — do no regular work. This is a permanent statute in every generation, wherever you live.
23.22: When you harvest your land, do not strip the corners or pick up the gleanings. Leave these for the poor and the foreigner. I am the LORD your God.
23.24: Tell Israel: the first day of the seventh month is a day of solemn rest, marked by trumpet blasts — a sacred assembly.
23.25: Do no regular work — bring a fire offering to the LORD.
23.27: The tenth day of the seventh month is the Day of Atonement — a sacred assembly. Fast and humble yourselves. Bring a fire offering to the LORD.
23.28: Do no work at all on that day — it is the Day of Atonement, when atonement is made for you before the LORD your God.
23.29: Anyone who does not fast and humble himself on that day shall be cut off from the people.
23.30: Anyone who works on that day — I will destroy that person from among my people.
23.31: Do no work of any kind. This is a permanent law in every generation, wherever you live.
23.32: It is a Sabbath of solemn rest — you shall fast. The day begins at sundown on the ninth and runs through the following sundown: evening to evening, you observe the Sabbath.
23.34: Tell Israel: the fifteenth day of the seventh month begins the Festival of Booths — seven days to the LORD.
23.35: On the first day there is a sacred assembly — do no ordinary work.
23.36: Present fire offerings to the LORD for seven days. The eighth day is a sacred assembly — bring a fire offering to the LORD. It is a solemn closing assembly; do no ordinary work.
23.37: These are the LORD's appointed times, which you are to declare as sacred assemblies. On each one, bring the required offerings — burnt offerings, grain offerings, fellowship sacrifices, and drink offerings — each in its proper season.
23.38: These appointed festivals come on top of the regular sabbaths, your personal gifts, vows, and freewill offerings to the LORD.
23.39: Once the harvest is gathered in — on the fifteenth of the seventh month — celebrate the LORD's festival for seven days: the first day is a rest-day, the eighth day also a rest-day.
23.40: On the first day, take the finest fruit, palm fronds, branches from leafy trees, and willows from the stream — and rejoice before the LORD your God for the full seven days.
23.41: Keep this festival to the LORD for seven days every year — a permanent law in every generation — always in the seventh month.
23.42: For seven days live in temporary shelters. Every native Israelite shall live in booths —
23.43: so that every generation remembers: when I brought Israel out of Egypt, they lived in temporary shelters in the wilderness. I am the LORD your God.
23.44: Moses announced to the Israelites all the LORD's appointed festivals.

24.2: Command Israel to bring you pure beaten olive oil for the light — to keep lamps burning without interruption.
24.3: In the tent of meeting, outside the curtain that screens the ark of the covenant, Aaron must tend the lamps every evening until morning before the LORD — a permanent responsibility in every generation.
24.4: He must keep the lamps of the pure gold lampstand in order before the LORD at all times.
24.5: Take fine flour and bake twelve loaves — two-tenths of an ephah each.
24.6: Arrange them in two rows of six on the pure gold table before the LORD.
24.7: Place pure frankincense on each row — it stands as a memorial offering to the LORD in place of the bread.
24.8: Every Sabbath the bread is renewed before the LORD — a standing covenant offering from Israel, renewed without interruption.
24.9: The bread belongs to Aaron and his sons, who eat it in the sacred precinct. It is most holy — their standing share from the LORD's offerings, a provision that never lapses.
24.10: A man of mixed parentage — his mother an Israelite, his father an Egyptian — went out among the Israelites and got into a fight with an Israelite.
24.11: During the fight he blasphemed the Name of the LORD and cursed it. He was brought to Moses. His mother was Shelomith, daughter of Dibri, of the tribe of Dan.
24.12: They held him in custody while they waited for the LORD's ruling on what to do.
24.14: Take the blasphemer outside the camp. Let all who heard him place their hands on his head — then let the whole congregation stone him.
24.15: Tell Israel: anyone who curses his God carries the guilt.
24.16: Whoever blasphemes the Name of the LORD shall be put to death — the whole congregation stones him. This applies equally to the foreigner and the native-born Israelite: blaspheme the Name, and die.
24.17: Anyone who takes a human life shall be put to death.
24.18: Anyone who kills another's animal must make restitution — life for life.
24.19: If anyone injures a neighbor — the same injury shall be returned to him:
24.20: fracture for fracture, eye for eye, tooth for tooth. Whatever injury he inflicted — the same measure is inflicted on him. The law establishes proportionality, not unlimited vengeance.
24.21: Kill an animal: pay for it. Kill a person: die for it.
24.22: One law applies to everyone — foreigner and native-born alike. I am the LORD your God.
24.23: Moses gave the command to Israel. They brought the blasphemer outside the camp and stoned him. Israel did exactly as the LORD had commanded Moses.

25.1: The LORD spoke to Moses on Mount Sinai:
25.2: Tell Israel: when you enter the land I am giving you, the land itself must keep a Sabbath to the LORD.
25.3: For six years sow your fields, prune your vines, and gather the harvest.
25.4: But in the seventh year the land has its Sabbath — a year of complete rest belonging to the LORD. Do not sow. Do not prune.
25.5: What grows on its own you must not harvest; the grapes of the untended vine you must not pick — it is the land's year of rest.
25.6: What the land yields on its own in that sabbath year is food for everyone — you, your servants, your hired workers, and the foreigner living among you.
25.7: Even your livestock and the wild animals in the land may eat what it produces.
25.8: Count seven Sabbath years — seven times seven — forty-nine years in all.
25.9: On the tenth day of the seventh month — the Day of Atonement — sound the ram's horn across the whole land.
25.10: Consecrate the fiftieth year. Proclaim freedom throughout the land to all its inhabitants. It is the Jubilee — every person returns to his ancestral land and to his family.
25.11: The fiftieth year is the Jubilee — do not sow, do not reap what grows on its own, do not gather grapes from untended vines.
25.12: The Jubilee is holy. Eat what the land freely gives — take it directly from the field.
25.13: In the Jubilee year, every person returns to his ancestral land.
25.14: When you buy or sell anything to a neighbor, deal honestly — do not exploit one another.
25.15: The price reflects the number of harvests left before the next Jubilee — more years remaining, higher the price; fewer years, lower.
25.16: Many harvests to come: higher price. Few harvests left: lower price. What is really being sold is the right to harvest — not the land itself.
25.17: Do not exploit one another in these transactions. Fear your God — I am the LORD your God.
25.18: Keep my statutes and carry out my rulings — then you will live safely in the land.
25.19: The land will yield its harvest, you will eat to the full, and you will live there in safety.
25.20: You may wonder: what will we eat in the seventh year, if we cannot sow or gather the harvest?
25.21: I will send my blessing in the sixth year so that the harvest is enough for three years.
25.22: You sow again in the eighth year. You eat from the stored harvest all through the eighth year and into the ninth — until the new crop is ready, the old sustains you.
25.23: The land may never be sold in permanent transfer — it belongs to me. You are resident aliens on my land, my tenants.
25.24: Across all the land you hold, the right of redemption must always remain open.
25.25: If a fellow Israelite falls into poverty and sells some of his land, his nearest kinsman may act as redeemer and buy it back.
25.26: If the man has no kinsman-redeemer but later earns enough to redeem it himself,
25.27: he calculates what the buyer has already received, pays back the balance, and reclaims his land.
25.28: If he cannot afford to buy it back, the land stays with the buyer until the Jubilee — then it is released and returns to its original owner.
25.29: A house inside a walled city: the seller has exactly one full year to buy it back.
25.30: If not redeemed within the year, the house becomes the buyer's permanently — in perpetuity — and it does not return in the Jubilee.
25.31: Houses in unwalled villages are treated like farmland — they can be redeemed at any time and return in the Jubilee.
25.32: Exception: the Levites may always redeem their houses in their designated cities, at any time.
25.33: If anyone purchases a house in a Levitical city, it returns in the Jubilee — those cities are the Levites' permanent inheritance among Israel.
25.34: The grazing land around the Levitical cities cannot be sold at all — it is the Levites' permanent inheritance.
25.35: If a fellow Israelite falls into poverty and can no longer support himself beside you, support him — whether he is a native or a resident foreigner — so he can go on living with you.
25.36: Do not charge him interest or demand a profit — fear your God instead, so that your neighbor can survive.
25.37: Do not lend money at interest. Do not sell him food at a markup.
25.38: I am the LORD your God, who brought you out of Egypt to give you Canaan and to be your God.
25.39: If a fellow Israelite falls so deep into poverty that he must sell himself to you, do not treat him as a slave.
25.40: Treat him as a hired worker, a fellow resident — he serves you until the Jubilee.
25.41: Then he goes free — he and his children — to return to his family and his ancestral land.
25.42: They are my servants — I brought them out of Egypt. They must not be sold as slaves.
25.43: Do not treat him harshly. Fear your God.
25.44: Your permanent slaves are to come from the surrounding nations — you may purchase them.
25.45: You may also purchase from the children of foreigners living among you — from those born in your land. They may become your property.
25.46: They can be passed on to your children as permanent property. But over your fellow Israelites, you must never exercise the harsh dominion of a slave-master.
25.47: If a foreigner living among you prospers while a fellow Israelite beside him falls into poverty and sells himself to that foreigner or to a member of his household —
25.48: even then he retains the right to be redeemed. A kinsman may redeem him —
25.49: his uncle, his uncle's son, or any near relative from his clan may do so. Or if he prospers, he may redeem himself.
25.50: He and his master calculate: from the year he was sold to the Jubilee, how many years remain? The redemption price is based on that period, treating each year as wages owed a hired worker.
25.51: If many years remain before the Jubilee, he refunds a larger portion of the purchase price.
25.52: If only a few years remain, the redemption payment is proportionally smaller.
25.53: He is to be treated as an annual wage-worker. The foreigner must not rule over him harshly — Israel is watching.
25.54: If he is not redeemed through any of these means, the Jubilee sets him free — along with his children.
25.55: The Israelites are my servants — mine. I brought them out of Egypt. I am the LORD your God.

26.1: Make no idols, erect no carved image or sacred pillar, set up no figured stone for worship. I am the LORD your God.
26.2: Keep my Sabbaths. Hold my sanctuary in reverence. I am the LORD.
26.3: If you live by my statutes and keep my commandments —
26.4: I will send rain in season. The land will yield its harvest. The trees will bear fruit.
26.5: The harvest will keep you busy until the grape harvest, and the grape harvest until planting time — abundance all year. You will eat your fill and live in your land without fear.
26.6: I will give peace to the land. You will lie down and no one will terrify you. I will drive dangerous animals from the land. No war will cross your borders.
26.7: When enemies come, you will pursue them — they will fall before you by the sword.
26.8: Five of you will rout a hundred. A hundred of you will put ten thousand to flight. Your enemies will fall by the sword before you.
26.9: I will look on you with favor, make you fruitful, multiply your numbers, and uphold my covenant with you.
26.10: You will still be eating from the previous year's harvest when the new crop forces it out — abundance upon abundance.
26.11: I will make my home among you and will not reject you.
26.12: I will walk among you. I will be your God. You will be my people.
26.13: I am the LORD your God, who brought you out of Egypt — out of slavery. I snapped the yoke from your neck and made you stand upright. That freedom is what is at stake.
26.14: But if you refuse to listen — if you will not carry out all these commandments —
26.15: if you despise my statutes, if you loathe my rulings so completely that you refuse all my commands and break my covenant —
26.16: then I will bring on you sudden terror — wasting disease and burning fever that dim your eyes and drain your life away. You will plant, but your enemies will eat the harvest.
26.17: I will set my face against you. You will be struck down before your enemies. Those who hate you will dominate you. You will run in panic when no one is even chasing you.
26.18: If after all this you still refuse to listen, I will multiply your punishment sevenfold.
26.19: I will shatter the pride your strength gives you. Your sky will be iron — no rain. Your earth will be bronze — no harvest.
26.20: All your labor will be wasted — the land yields nothing, the trees bear nothing.
26.21: If you keep defying me — still refusing to listen — I will strike you again, sevenfold, fitting the punishment to the sin.
26.22: I will unleash wild animals against you. They will tear your children away, decimate your livestock, shrink your numbers — and the roads will go empty.
26.23: If these things still do not bring you to repentance — if you continue to defy me —
26.24: then I myself will be your adversary and multiply your punishment sevenfold for your sins.
26.25: I will bring war against you — war that avenges the broken covenant. When you crowd into your cities for safety, I will send plague among you, and you will be handed over to the enemy.
26.26: I will cut off your food supply. Ten women will share one oven — bread rationed out by weight — and you will eat but never feel full.
26.27: If even this does not bring you to obedience — if you still defy me —
26.28: then I will confront you in fury — I myself — and punish you sevenfold for your sins.
26.29: You will eat the flesh of your own sons and daughters — the final horror of famine and siege.
26.30: I will demolish your high places, cut down your incense altars, and pile your corpses on the rubble of your idols. I will reject you utterly.
26.31: I will reduce your cities to rubble and make your sanctuaries desolate. I will no longer receive your offerings — their fragrance means nothing to me now.
26.32: The land itself will become a wasteland — so utterly destroyed that even your conquerors will be shocked by it.
26.33: I will scatter you among the nations and pursue you with the sword. Your land will lie in ruin. Your cities will be empty ruins.
26.34: Only then, while the land lies empty and you are in exile, will it enjoy all the sabbath years you denied it. The land gets its rest — at your expense.
26.35: Every year of exile is a sabbath for the land — the rest it was owed but never given while you lived on it.
26.36: Those who survive among your enemies will be paralyzed with fear. The rustling of a leaf will send them running as if from a sword — they will stumble and fall though no one is chasing them.
26.37: They will trip over each other in the rout — as if fleeing a sword, with no enemy in sight. You will be unable to make a stand.
26.38: You will waste away among the nations. The foreign lands will swallow you up.
26.39: The survivors will waste away in exile, weighed down by their own sins — and by the accumulated guilt of their ancestors.
26.40: But if they confess — their sin and their fathers' sin, the faithlessness they showed me, the defiance they lived in —
26.41: and acknowledge that my judgment drove them into exile — if their stubborn, uncircumcised hearts finally humble themselves and they accept the consequences of their sin —
26.42: then I will remember my covenant — with Jacob, with Isaac, with Abraham — and I will remember the land.
26.43: The land will lie empty, enjoying its sabbath rest — the rest they never gave it. They will pay for their sin. The cause was plain: they despised my rulings and loathed my statutes.
26.44: And yet — even in exile — I will not reject them or loathe them to the point of annihilation. I will not break my covenant with them. I am the LORD their God.
26.45: For their sake I will remember the covenant I made with their ancestors — whom I brought out of Egypt before the watching nations, to be their God. I am the LORD.
26.46: These are the statutes, the rulings, and the laws the LORD established with Israel at Mount Sinai through Moses.

27.2: Tell Israel: when a man makes a special vow — dedicating a person to the LORD — the following valuations apply:
27.3: A male between twenty and sixty years of age: fifty shekels of silver by the sanctuary standard.
27.4: A female of the same age range: thirty shekels.
27.5: Ages five to twenty — male: twenty shekels. Female: ten shekels.
27.6: One month to five years — male: five shekels. Female: three shekels.
27.7: Sixty years and above — male: fifteen shekels. Female: ten shekels.
27.8: If the person who made the vow cannot afford the standard amount, he comes before the priest, who sets a valuation he can actually pay.
27.9: If the vow is of a clean animal that could be offered to the LORD, every such animal given becomes holy.
27.10: He must not swap it — not a good animal for a bad one, not a bad for a good. If he does make a substitution, both the original and the substitute become holy.
27.11: If the vow involves an unclean animal that cannot be offered, he brings it before the priest,
27.12: who assesses its value — good quality or poor. The priest's word is final.
27.13: If the owner wants to buy it back, he must pay the assessed value plus twenty percent.
27.14: If a man dedicates his house to the LORD as a holy thing, the priest assesses it — high quality or low. The priest's valuation stands.
27.15: If he wants to buy it back, he pays the assessed value plus twenty percent — and the house returns to him.
27.16: If a man dedicates part of his family's farmland to the LORD, the valuation is based on how much seed it takes to sow it — fifty shekels of silver per homer of barley seed.
27.17: If he dedicates it right after the Jubilee, the full valuation applies.
27.18: If he dedicates it later in the jubilee cycle, the priest calculates the years remaining to the Jubilee and reduces the valuation accordingly.
27.19: If he wants to redeem the dedicated field, he pays the assessed value plus twenty percent — and the field is securely his again.
27.20: If he does not redeem it — or if he has already sold it to someone else — the option to redeem is gone.
27.21: When the Jubilee comes, that field becomes permanently holy to the LORD — like land placed under the ban. It becomes priestly property.
27.22: If a man dedicates to the LORD a purchased field (not his family's ancestral land),
27.23: the priest calculates its value based on the years remaining to the Jubilee, and the man pays that amount to the LORD as a holy offering on the spot.
27.24: At the Jubilee the field reverts to the person he bought it from — the original owner of that land.
27.25: All monetary valuations in these laws use the sanctuary standard: twenty gerahs to the shekel.
27.26: The firstborn of any animal cannot be dedicated — it already belongs to the LORD. You cannot give what is already his.
27.27: If the firstborn is an unclean animal, it may be redeemed at its assessed value plus twenty percent. If not redeemed, the priest sells it at the assessed price.
27.28: But the cherem — anything irrevocably devoted to the LORD, whether person, animal, or ancestral land — cannot be sold or bought back. What is placed under the ban is most holy to the LORD.
27.29: A person placed under the ban of destruction cannot be redeemed. He shall be put to death.
27.30: A tenth of the land's produce — grain or fruit — belongs to the LORD. It is holy.
27.31: If someone wants to redeem part of his tithe in cash, he pays its value plus twenty percent.
27.32: For herd and flock, every tenth animal that passes under the counting rod becomes holy to the LORD.
27.33: The owner must not select which animal — picking only the best or worst. Whatever is tenth belongs to the LORD. If he tries to make a swap, both animals become holy and neither can be redeemed.
27.34: These are the commandments the LORD gave to Moses for Israel on Mount Sinai.
